These functions use standard AUR practices: downloading a tarball from the project's GitHub release and cloning a Git repository from the same upstream.

The variables `_commit_alpm` and `_cksum_octopi` are set at the top with defaults and used to pin the Git commit and tarball hash, respectively. The use of `SKIP` for checksums is appropriate here because the Git source is pinned to a commit, and VCS sources traditionally use `SKIP`. The tarball checksum is conditionally set to the provided hash unless overridden, which is a common and safe pattern.

No command substitution, network requests, or file operations occur outside of the standard `source` array population. The functions `_prepare_*` and `_build_*` are defined but not executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare --printsrcinfo`, so their contents are irrelevant for this safety gate. Sourcing the PKGBUILD only executes the global logic, which is limited to variable assignments and safe function calls that only modify `source` and `sha256sums`.

Thus, running the specified `makepkg` commands will only fetch the declared sources from trusted upstream URLs without executing any build or prepare steps, posing no security risk during this phase.
</details>
<summary>

</summary>

data.reasoning: Both models reviewed the same set of files and found no malicious indicators. The only disagreement stems from a technical API error (empty response content) when deepseek tried to assess GenerateFindModule.cmake — not from any actual security finding. Notably, even deepseek's truncated reasoning trace for that file concluded it was a benign CMake utility that writes Find modules with no network, eval, or obfuscation. Qwen successfully reviewed the same file and marked it SAFE with sound reasoning. The PKGBUILD sources from pinned, trusted upstream GitHub repos (aarnt/octopi tarball with checksum, alpm_octopi_utils git), uses standard cmake/make builds, and has an empty install script. No risk vectors remain unaddressed.
data.disagreement_analysis: The disagreement is artificial: deepseek's 'inconclusive' verdict was caused by an OpenRouter API error returning null content (finish_reason='error') for GenerateFindModule.cmake, not by a substantive security concern. Its own reasoning trace indicated the file was safe. Qwen reviewed the identical file without error and judged it SAFE.
learnings: Handle API/transport errors (finish_reason='error', null content) distinctly from genuine 'inconclusive' security verdicts — these should trigger an automatic retry rather than degrading the overall package verdict.
When a model produces a populated reasoning trace but null final content, salvage the verdict from the reasoning text or re-issue the request before flagging inconclusive.

Cross-model agreement on all reviewed files except one that failed due to a transport error should not be surfaced as a genuine 'disagreement (inconclusive)' — the adjudication layer should recognize and down-weight infrastructure-induced inconclusiveness.

Both models converged on an identical, appropriate file selection (PKGBUILD, install scripts, cmake/make build files, desktop entry) — file prioritization worked well here; translation .ts files were correctly assessed as inert data.
Consider deduplicating retries on cheaper models when a single file fails so a 10/11 success doesn't poison the aggregate result.
